sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'moves player 4x4 avatar right by 6 cols to next chamber (VERIFIED steps 1,3,4)', 'DOWN': 'only shrinks HUD timer at [8,20] (step 2); untested at other positions', 'LEFT': 'untested', 'UP': 'untested', 'SPACE': 'untested'}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_square_black_center': 'player avatar, 4x4 blue square with 1 black center pixel, 24 pixels total', 'blue_square_blue_center': 'target/goal, 6x6 blue square with 1 blue center pixel, 19 pixels, at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]', 'white_paths': 'maze corridors forming S-shaped path through black walls', 'red_path': 'red colored path on right side of maze, 82 pixels', 'white_square': 'white 5x5 square at [14,20]-[18,24], possibly another target or obstacle'}
- HUD map: {'row_63': 'blue timer strip, shrinks by 1 pixel per action (64→63→...)'}
- Win pattern: Reach the blue target square at bottom-right with the player avatar
Current level:
- World model: Maze with chambers connected by white corridors. Player is 4x4 blue square that jumps between chambers. Chambers in top row at cols 14, 20, 26, 32 (spaced 6 apart). White path forms S-shape: top-left → top-right → down right side → left → down → right to bottom. Player currently at [8,32] after 3 RIGHTs.
- Goal model: Navigate player from start (top-left) through maze chambers to blue target at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]
- Recent findings: ['Player moved: [8,14]→[8,20]→[8,26]→[8,32] with RIGHT', 'DOWN at [8,20] only changed HUD timer, no player movement', 'Player is now at [8,32] near red path area']
- Open questions: ['Does DOWN work at [8,32] (just tested, awaiting result)?', 'What is the full chamber connectivity graph?', 'Can LEFT reverse movement?', 'What does SPACE do?']
- Plan: Test DOWN at [8,32]. If it works, continue following the S-shaped white corridor path: down right side, then left, then down, then right to target. If not, try RIGHT more or LEFT back to find working DOWN position.
- Failed probes: ['DOWN at [8,20] - no player movement, only HUD timer changed (step 2)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE changed the board. Looking at the image, the player moved from [8,38] to [8,14] - it teleported back to the leftmost chamber! The top-left indicator also changed from blue to gray. So SPACE seems to reset the player to the start position.
Let me verify the player's new position and rethink the strategy. The player can only move RIGHT in the top row, and DOWN doesn't work. Maybe I need to go to a specific chamber where DOWN works, or maybe the maze works differently than I thought.
Wait - looking more carefully at the image, the player is now at [8,14] again (leftmost chamber). SPACE reset the player. Let me try a different approach - maybe I should go to the second chamber [8,20] and try DOWN from there again, or try LEFT from the current position.
Actually, let me reconsider the maze structure. The white path in the maze goes:
- From top-left area, right across
- Then down the right side
- Then left across the middle
- Then down
- Then right to bottom
Maybe the player doesn't follow the white path directly but instead jumps between "chambers" or "nodes" in the maze. The chambers I've seen are at row 8 (top row). Maybe there are chambers at other rows too, and I need to find the right one to jump down from.
Let me try going LEFT from current position to see if that works, and also try going back to [8,20] and trying DOWN again. But wait, I already tried DOWN at [8,20] and it didn't work.
Let me try a different approach: go to [8,26] (third chamber) and try DOWN there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE reset player to [8,14]. Check position and try different approach.
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24 and n['children']:
            return n
    return None

ap = find_player(last_transition.after_frame.segmentation)
print(f"Player after SPACE: {ap['boundary'][0] if ap else '?'}")

# Go to [8,26] (third chamber) and try DOWN there
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "moves player right between chambers (6 cols each)", "DOWN": "does not move player in top row", "SPACE": "resets player to start [8,14]"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_square_black_center": "player avatar (4x4)", "blue_square_blue_center": "target/goal", "bottom_blue_strip": "HUD timer"}, "hud_map": {"row_63": "blue timer strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player reset to [8,14] by SPACE. Top row chambers at cols 14,20,26,32,38.", "goal_model": "Navigate to blue target at bottom right", "recent_findings": ["SPACE resets player to start"], "open_questions": ["How to move down? Maybe need specific chamber?"], "current_plan": "Try DOWN at [8,26] chamber", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["DOWN at [8,20]", "DOWN at [8,32]", "DOWN at [8,38]", "SPACE resets"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
